skill.name: python-testing-patterns
skill.description: Guide Python testing with pytest patterns, fixtures, mocking, async tests, database tests, coverage, and CI examples.
skill.summary: Build maintainable Python test suites with pytest, fixtures, mocks, coverage, and CI patterns.
skill.author: ActiveInferenceInstitute

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many severe patterns, but review shows they are almost entirely Markdown tutorial examples rather than active code. No malicious intent, prompt injection, or data exfiltration evidence was found; residual risk is limited to users copying example commands, URLs, CI actions, or placeholder credentials without adapting them.

low_findings.title: Static command findings are Markdown fences
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby or shell backtick detections are Markdown code fences around tutorial examples. They are not executable Ruby backtick calls in the skill file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding lines show fenced Markdown blocks and comments, not runtime code. The skill is a single Markdown guide with no executable script file.

low_findings.title: Network examples are mocked or illustrative
low_findings.description: The requests and hardcoded URLs appear in pytest mocking examples and documentation links. They do not exfiltrate data, but users should avoid running copied examples against real services without review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The requests calls are patched in tests or return synthetic data. Hardcoded URLs are example domains or documentation links, not suspicious endpoints.

low_findings.title: Example environment and credential placeholders
low_findings.description: The skill includes DATABASE_URL, api_key, and production-key examples for monkeypatch and fixture patterns. These are placeholders, but copying them can normalize hardcoded secrets in tests.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The values are clearly tutorial placeholders and localhost connection strings. The concern is copy-paste hygiene, not confirmed credential theft.

low_findings.title: CI example runs commands and uploads coverage
low_findings.description: The GitHub Actions sample installs packages, runs pytest, and uses Codecov. This is normal testing guidance, but users should pin actions and review third-party CI services before adoption.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The workflow is a standard CI example with explicit run steps and third-party actions. It is not malicious, but it can affect supply-chain posture if copied unchanged.

dangerous_patterns.title: Network examples are mocked or illustrative
dangerous_patterns.description: The requests and hardcoded URLs appear in pytest mocking examples and documentation links. They do not exfiltrate data, but users should avoid running copied examples against real services without review.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The requests calls are patched in tests or return synthetic data. Hardcoded URLs are example domains or documentation links, not suspicious endpoints.

dangerous_patterns.title: Example environment and credential placeholders
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes DATABASE_URL, api_key, and production-key examples for monkeypatch and fixture patterns. These are placeholders, but copying them can normalize hardcoded secrets in tests.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The values are clearly tutorial placeholders and localhost connection strings. The concern is copy-paste hygiene, not confirmed credential theft.

dangerous_patterns.title: CI example runs commands and uploads coverage
dangerous_patterns.description: The GitHub Actions sample installs packages, runs pytest, and uses Codecov. This is normal testing guidance, but users should pin actions and review third-party CI services before adoption.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The workflow is a standard CI example with explicit run steps and third-party actions. It is not malicious, but it can affect supply-chain posture if copied unchanged.

content.value_statement: Python projects often lack consistent tests, fixtures, and CI coverage. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code practical pytest patterns for reliable test design.
actual_capabilities: Explains pytest unit test structure with arrange, act, and assert patterns.
Shows fixture scopes, teardown behavior, and shared conftest usage.
Demonstrates parameterized tests, exception tests, and property-based tests.
Covers mocking requests, async test patterns, temporary files, and database sessions.
Provides coverage, pytest configuration, markers, and GitHub Actions CI examples.
limitations: It is an instructional Markdown guide, not an executable testing framework.
Examples use placeholder names and must be adapted to each project.
It does not run tests or inspect a repository by itself.
CI and network examples require security review before production use.

use_cases.title: Create a First Pytest Suite
use_cases.description: Use the basic patterns to add clear unit tests, fixtures, and exception checks to a new Python module.

use_cases.title: Improve Service Test Coverage
use_cases.description: Apply mocking, async tests, database fixtures, and temporary file patterns to cover service behavior without fragile dependencies.

use_cases.title: Standardize CI Testing
use_cases.description: Use the coverage, marker, configuration, and GitHub Actions examples to align team testing workflows.

prompt_templates.title: Add Basic Tests
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to design pytest tests for my Python function. Include clear test names, normal cases, edge cases, and exception cases.

prompt_templates.title: Design Fixtures
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to create pytest fixtures for my test suite. Include setup, teardown, fixture scope, and guidance for avoiding shared state.

prompt_templates.title: Mock External Services
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to test code that calls external APIs. Show how to mock requests, assert call arguments, and avoid real network calls.

prompt_templates.title: Build a CI Testing Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Use this skill to propose a full Python testing strategy with unit tests, integration tests, markers, coverage thresholds, and CI workflow guidance.

output_examples.input: A small calculator module needs tests for arithmetic behavior and invalid inputs.
output_examples.output: A pytest test plan with descriptive cases for addition, subtraction, division, zero division, and parameterized numeric inputs.

output_examples.input: An API client should be tested without sending real HTTP requests.
output_examples.output: A mocking approach that replaces network calls, checks returned data, verifies request arguments, and covers error responses.

output_examples.input: A team wants consistent test execution in CI.
output_examples.output: A testing workflow outline with dependency installation, pytest execution, coverage reporting, markers, and review notes for third-party actions.

best_practices: Adapt example values to safe local test data before adding them to a repository.
Mock external dependencies so unit tests stay deterministic and offline.
Use coverage as a guide while prioritizing meaningful behavior checks.
anti_patterns: Do not copy placeholder secrets or production-like keys into committed tests.
Do not let tests depend on live external APIs unless they are marked integration tests.
Do not chase coverage numbers with vague tests that do not assert behavior.

faq.question: Does this skill run pytest automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides testing patterns and examples that an AI assistant or developer can apply.

faq.question: Can it help with existing Python projects?
faq.answer: Yes. Use it to plan fixtures, mocks, parameterized tests, coverage settings, and CI test workflows.

faq.question: Does it require network access?
faq.answer: No. Network-related examples are for mocking API clients and should not call real services in unit tests.

faq.question: Are the database URLs real credentials?
faq.answer: No. They are illustrative localhost and in-memory examples, but teams should replace them with safe test configuration.

faq.question: Can it support async Python code?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes pytest-asyncio style examples for async functions, async fixtures, and concurrent tasks.

faq.question: Is it suitable for CI setup?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes coverage and GitHub Actions examples, which should be reviewed and pinned for production use.
